coredata.dc:description:
Because of their potential applications in electricity and photonics, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with intriguing physical characteristics have attracted a lot of attention. Here we use density functional theory (DFT) with the B3LYP functional associated with the 6–31++G(d,p) basis to explore the effect of the nitrogen atom on the optoelectronic, nonlinear optical, and thermodynamic properties of dibenzocoronene (DBC) isomers. The pristine molecules of the DBC isomers have HOMO-LUMO energy gaps of 3.39
eV
(
DBC a
), 3.39
eV
(
DBC b
) and 3.65
eV
(
DBC c
). Inserting a nitrogen atom into the DBC molecular structure considerably reduces the energy gap between these two orbitals. The materials thus obtained after nitrogen doping are
DBC a
-N,
DBC b
-N, and
DBC c
-N and have respective gap energies of 2.05, 1.80, and 1.65
eV
(in other words, less than 3
eV
). It can therefore be deduced that the compounds studied will be used as semiconductors in electronics. The static polarizabilities α of the N-doped DBC isomers obtained are 442.960, 443.523, and 455.657 au, respectively, each of these values being 4 times higher than that of the organic reference molecule for non-linear optical properties (para-nitroaniline pNA α = 114.7 au), while the first-order hyperpolarizabilities β obtained are 2689.838, 5572.137, and 12717.472 au, respectively. These values are approximately 2.5, 5, and 12 times higher than those of para-nitroaniline (β = 1072.44 au). Our results confirm that the investigated DBC nitrogen derivatives are good non-linear optical materials for photonics, data storage, dynamic imaging, optical signal processing, and computer systems. All the calculated thermodynamic properties show that the proposed molecules can be easily produced. This manuscript thus stands out as a good compass in the rational design of organic semiconductors for efficient electronic devices.

head.ce:title: The impact of the nitrogen atom on the optoelectronic, nonlinear optical, and thermodynamic properties of graphene quantum dots derived from dibenzocoronene: A DFT investigation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Because of their potential applications in electricity and photonics, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with intriguing physical characteristics have attracted a lot of attention. Here we use density functional theory (DFT) with the B3LYP functional associated with the 6–31++G(d,p) basis to explore the effect of the nitrogen atom on the optoelectronic, nonlinear optical, and thermodynamic properties of dibenzocoronene (DBC) isomers. The pristine molecules of the DBC isomers have HOMO-LUMO energy gaps of 3.39
eV
(
DBC a
), 3.39
eV
(
DBC b
) and 3.65
eV
(
DBC c
). Inserting a nitrogen atom into the DBC molecular structure considerably reduces the energy gap between these two orbitals. The materials thus obtained after nitrogen doping are
DBC a
-N,
DBC b
-N, and
DBC c
-N and have respective gap energies of 2.05, 1.80, and 1.65
eV
(in other words, less than 3
eV
). It can therefore be deduced that the compounds studied will be used as semiconductors in electronics. The static polarizabilities α of the N-doped DBC isomers obtained are 442.960, 443.523, and 455.657 au, respectively, each of these values being 4 times higher than that of the organic reference molecule for non-linear optical properties (para-nitroaniline pNA α = 114.7 au), while the first-order hyperpolarizabilities β obtained are 2689.838, 5572.137, and 12717.472 au, respectively. These values are approximately 2.5, 5, and 12 times higher than those of para-nitroaniline (β = 1072.44 au). Our results confirm that the investigated DBC nitrogen derivatives are good non-linear optical materials for photonics, data storage, dynamic imaging, optical signal processing, and computer systems. All the calculated thermodynamic properties show that the proposed molecules can be easily produced. This manuscript thus stands out as a good compass in the rational design of organic semiconductors for efficient electronic devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
There are numerous uses for materials with broadband emission band absorption in photodetectors, broadband modulators, solar cells, bioimaging, and other fields. These materials span the ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared portions of the solar spectrum. Furthermore, they can be highly helpful in applications involving high harmonic generators, multiphoton imaging, and optical parametric oscillations because of their non-linear optical features [1,2]. It is therefore highly desirable to discover novel materials that possess both absorption and nonlinear optical activity in order to simultaneously change the UV-VIS and IR active optical ranges. Consequently, modified GQDs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) seem to be attractive materials for such optical activities [3]. They can take the place of hazardous inorganic quantum dots because of their improved photostability, biocompatibility, and inexpensive preparation. Constrained graphene materials, or GQDs, come in a variety of topologies. Graphene is a crystalline allotropic form of
s p
2
bonded carbon stacked in a honeycomb network; its zero bandgap limits its use in optoelectronic applications because it emits no light. However, GQDs show a high rate of absorption and have become popular materials for fluorescence in the visible, ultraviolet, and even infrared spectrums [4]. Many experimental and theoretical studies have examined the high-throughput activity of GQDs with varying sizes, shapes, and functions [4–7]. This is a development in the understanding of the nonlinear activity of GQDs that depends on form and size. For usage in organic electronics, the efficiency of charge conduction, photoluminescence, and material qualities, taking into account size, shape, steric, and π−π electrostatic interactions between nearby molecules, all have a significant impact [6]. To fully realize the promise of these systems, it is therefore necessary to be able to design and manufacture new polyfused aromatic molecules. For highly symmetric polyfused aromatics like linear acenes, perylenes, coronene, and their derivatives, Scholl, Clar, Müllen et al. have devised a variety of synthetic methods [8]. A novel family of semiconductor materials based on coronene was developed in 1932 by Scholl and Meyer. In order to determine the bond lengths in the molecule, J. Monteath Robertson and James Trotter [9] conducted a thorough X-ray analysis of the crystal and molecular structure of dibenzocoronene (
DBC a
) in 1961. A new series of polyfused aromatics based on coronene was successfully synthesized in 2015 by Sushil Kumar et al. [10], one of whose DBC (dibenzocoronene) derivatives provided an average hole mobility of 0.42
c m
2
/V.s. The same year, Charma Yamijala and aL [7]. investigated the impact of edge structures, size, and shape on the electrical characteristics of GQDs, particularly dibenzocoronenes, using DFT. It was demonstrated that the most stable GQDs are circular and triangular shapes with zigzag edges, and that the gap energy of the GQDs under study ranged from 0 to 3 eV depending on shape, amount of carbon atoms, and edge type.

Graphene's superior thermal, mechanical, and electrical qualities make it a great material for electronic devices [11–16]. Graphene's zero bandgaps, however, restrict its use in semi-conducting applications [17–19]. Consequently, a variety of changes, such as vacancy generation and covalent and noncovalent functionalization, are suggested in the literature to improve the optoelectronic behaviour of these materials [20–26]. Covalent functionalization, that is, substituting heteroatoms (nitrogen, boron), or metal atoms for one or more carbon atoms, as well as reduction of the material into quantum dots, are two of the suggested techniques that work well for modifying the bandgaps of materials like graphene [27–30].

Prior studies, including the functionalization and nitrogen doping of GQDs, have demonstrated good optoelectronic property tuning. Accordingly, doping is more stable with nitrogen than with sulfur, and doping near the edge is more advantageous than doping centered, as demonstrated by Dominique Mombrú et al. (2019) [31] using DFT calculations on the electronic and optical properties of graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur atoms. Furthermore, because there are more strains in centered doping, it is more challenging, but edge doping is more successful at relieving stress because it permits repair and rearrangement surrounding nearby atoms. The electrical distribution of GQDs can be fine-tuned by doping them with foreign atoms to improve their electronic and optical properties. Nitrogen (N) [32], boron (B) [33], and phosphorus (P) [34] are among the heteroatoms that have been investigated for this purpose. N-GQDs are the most studied because the N atom has a comparable atomic size and an extra-valence electron to bond with carbon atoms. Studies have shown that introducing N atoms to GQDs can effectively adjust their intrinsic properties for advanced device applications [35–39]. For instance, Jianguang Feng et al. (2021) [40] reported that N-doping patterns associated with B, P, and S atoms can give GQDs a wide range of new optical properties and electronic structures. Peng Cui and Yuan Xue [41] conducted a detailed analysis of non-radiative recombination in pristine GQDs and N-GQDs with different N functionalities, namely pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, using the Fermi golden rule approach. The study found that N doping alters the hybridization of boundary molecular orbitals in N-GQDs, reducing the HOMO-LUMO gap and increasing the electron-hole separation distances compared to pristine GQDs. Boron and nitrogen atoms, which are similar in size to carbon, are ideal candidates for graphene substitutional doping [42,43].

Recently, Misbah Asif et al. (2023) [44] reported boron-rich triphenylene-based graphene quantum dots with outstanding nonlinear optical activity, utilizing covalent modifications. Using the same methodology, Aloumko et al. (2023) [45] enhanced the optical, optoelectronic, and nonlinear electronic reactivity of graphene
C 45
H 15
doped with nitrogen, boron, and titanium dioxide. As previously mentioned, nitrogen atoms are widely used for heteroatomic doping of GQDs. Therefore, our motivation for this work stems from previous research on N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) [46].

This study focused on the functionalization of dibenzocoronene isomers (Fig. 1 ) with nitrogen atoms using theoretical methods. The effects on shape and edge structures were also investigated. The work uses DFT calculations to provide the nonlinear optical, optoelectronic, and thermodynamic properties of the dibenzocoronene molecule and its nitrogen derivatives. We study key factors such as geometric structures, electronic energies, vibrational frequencies, molecular orbitals, electrostatic potential, and absorption spectra. We expect to propose new functionalized molecules for possible implications in electronics and optoelectronics.

The Gaussian 16 software package [47] was used to simulate the DFT calculations [48,49]. The modeled structures were generated and visualized using GaussView 06 [50]. The DFT method using the hybrid functional B3LYP associated with the 6–31++G(d,p) basis is known for its accuracy in this type of research and is used for geometric optimization and the study of electronic properties [51–60]. In addition, the GaussSum software [61] generated density of state (DOS) spectra to explain the change in HOMO-LUMO energy gaps during nitrogen doping. The properties of excited states have been explored by TD-DFT theory [62,63] using the CAM-B3LYP functional [64] and the 6–31++G(d,p) basis. As for the Multiwfn3.7 software [65], it extracted the linear and non-linear optical (NLO) properties, such as the dipole moment μ, static polarizabilities α, anisotropic polarizabilities Δα, and first-order hyperpolarizabilities β, while the Kisthelp program [66] analyzed all the thermodynamic properties of the molecules studied.

It has been demonstrated that the MEP is remarkably effective at characterizing non-covalent interactions, particularly those involving hydrogen bonds. We start this section by presenting the procedure used to insert nitrogen inside the DBC skeleton. As stated by previous indications, Hongqi Sun et al. [76] signaled that an N-doping level of about 5 % can be employed as a criteron. Thus, equation (24) can be employed to evaluate the number of nitrogen atoms to insert inside the carbon skeleton.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometries of nitrogenous derivatives of the most probable and energetically stable DBCs
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to determine the most energetically advantageous configuration, the total energy of DBCs doped with 3.1 % N was computed for each of the three systems under study that involved switching out a carbon atom for a nitrogen atom. The configurations with
DBC a
-N,
DBC b
-N, and
DBC c
-N total energies showed the highest interest, making them the most likely and stable among them. Fig. 5 depicts these arrangements, which will be the focus of the remainder of our investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometric parameters of the compounds studied and their nitrogen derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: Here, we study the effect of nitrogen doping on some valence angles and some bond length between the nitrogen-containing benzene ring of DBC molecules.

B3LYP/6–31++G(d,p) is used to extract geometric parameters of the studied molecules, such as bond lengths and valence angles. Analysis is done on how the nitrogen atom affects these properties. As seen in Table 1 , the bond lengths and bond angles calculated at the B3LYP/6–31++G(d,p) level of theory show a close match with the available experimental data [9]. This indicates that the optimization method used for the studied molecules is appropriate, and the related conclusions are heading towards being coherent and realistic. For instance, the obtained valence angle A(C1–C2–C3) for
DBC a
has a value of 120.95°, which shows a difference of 2.05° from the experimental data. As can be seen in the same table, the bond C1–N2 formed when nitrogen replaces carbon atom C2 has a value of 1.377 Å, indicating a minor increase of 0.007 Å. The angle A(C1–N2–C3) has a value of 122.98°; in comparison to the basic system, this angle has increased by 2.03°. The bond (C1–C2) and angle A(C1–C2–C3) in the
DBC b
system are, respectively, 1.371 Å and 120.95°. The bond (N1–C2) and angle A(N1–C2–C3) obtained by replacing carbon C1 with nitrogen are, respectively, 1.383 Å and 120.20°. With nitrogen doping, we have a minor increase in the bond length of 0.012 Å and a decrease in the angle of 0.75°. In conclusion, the bond (C3–C4) and the angle A(C4–C3–C2) for the
D B C
c
under study are 1.383 Å and 119.68°, respectively. The bond (C3–N4) and angle A(N4–C3–C2) obtained by replacing carbon C4 with nitrogen are, respectively, 1.386 Å and 119.22°. We see a modest rise in the bond of 0.003 Å and a decrease in the angle of 0.46° with the doping. Hence, throughout the whole benzene cycle, the addition of the nitrogen atom modifies the valence angles and interatomic distances. It is our opinion that substantial electronic delocalizations of these nitrogen-doped molecules are necessary in order to influence the optical and electrical characteristics of the recently developed materials.

The values of the parameters acquired and those experimental from the
DBC a
[9] show a small difference based on this examination of the values of the valence angles and the table of interatomic distances. Comparing the various nitrogen derivatives to their basic systems, it was possible to see a minor increase or decrease in specific valence angles and interatomic distances across the benzene ring due to the action of nitrogen doping. By delocalizing the π bonds along the benzene nucleus of the DBCs, nitrogen can readily enter the carbon matrix and facilitate the transmission of electric charges, as demonstrated by this [69]. Nitrogen has a radius that is similar to that of carbon. The presence of a delocalized π-system across the entire network is an intriguing characteristic, as it gives conjugated systems their original properties and is the origin of conduction in organic materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optoelectronic properties of DBCs and their derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: Table 2 shows the HOMO, LUMO, gap, and absorption wavelengths of DBCs and their derivatives.

Both the semiconductor nature and the electrical stability of materials can be discussed using the gap energy, or (
E gap
). Additionally, it makes it easier to distinguish between molecules' chemical reactivity [79,80]. The gap energy values of the base molecules, measured with the B3LYP functional, are 3.39 eV (
DBC a
), 3.39 eV (
DBC b
), and 3.65 eV (
DBC c
). The nitrogen derivatives' gap energy values are 2.05 eV (
DBC a
-N), 1.80 eV (
DBC b
-N), and 1.65 eV (
DBC c
-N). We can see from these energy values that doping virgin molecules with nitrogen greatly enhances their conductive qualities. With a lower gap energy than the other derivatives, the nitrogen derivative
DBC c
-N is more reactive. The gap energies of nitrogen-doped molecules are below 3 eV, which means that these nitrogen compounds are good semiconductors. The absorption wavelengths of the neutral molecules
DBC a
,
DBC b
, and
DBC c
are 365.78 nm, 365.78 nm, and 339.73 nm, respectively. These values are below 400 nm, so undoped compounds absorb in the ultraviolet. Since the absorption wavelength values (i.e., 604.88 nm, 688.89 nm, and 751.52 nm, respectively) of the nitrogen-doped molecules
DBC a
-N,
DBC b
-N, and
DBC c
-N are well above 400 nm, the latter can therefore absorb in the visible range. We draw the conclusion that the addition of a nitrogen atom to the carbon matrix changed the compounds' properties, resulting in a significant decrease in the band gap and an expansion of the range of light absorption. Thus, nitrogen presents a promising option for doping to enhance nonlinear optoelectronic and optical characteristics. The new materials that are being suggested can be used in photonic and electronic applications such as light-emitting diodes, solar cells, field-effect transistors, and bioimaging.

When determining the distinctive vibration frequencies of the functional groups in different organic compounds, infrared spectra are useful for analyzing and measuring these wavelengths, or frequencies. To demonstrate the stability of molecules, vibrational frequency calculations are done. Based on the analysis of these spectra, it is evident that our molecules do not exhibit any imaginary frequencies, and the molecular models are stable. Then the minima on the potential energy surface are obtained [81]. With reference to the 6–31++G (d,p) basis set, the B3LYP is employed for the vibrational analysis of the infrared frequencies. Fig. 6 displays the infrared spectra of all the molecules investigated here, and Table 3 collects some selected vibrational modes for the studied molecules. It is important to note that no experimental IR spectra are currently available for the molecules under investigation. Nevertheless, the N–H vibration of N-doped molecules has been analyzed in comparison with established standard experimental values, as detailed in this section. It can be seen that the IR spectra for the studied molecules exhibit absorption bands for which we have located some specific pecks, principally centered at 3230, 3649, and 3787 cm−1 and assigned to the N–H stretching. This result coincides well with the known experimental N–H stretching (about 3400 cm−1 [82]). In addition, similar observations were conducted by Ref. [83] in the N-doped graphene quantum dots, for which they located N–H stretching at 3146 cm−1 for their molecule of interest. All this indicates a successful nitrogen functionalization of the pristine DBC molecules.
The bond frequencies and vibration modes observed in Table 3 are consistent with experimental data [82].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of doping on absorption spectra
ce:section.ce:para:
For a better understanding of electron transitions, we employed the CAM-B3LYP/6–31++G(d,p) level to compute the absorption spectra of DBC molecules and their N-doped derivatives. It is well known that the CAM-B3LYP functional is suitable for UV–vis analysis as it was developed to minimize errors upon charge-transfer energies [84]. Fig. 7 (a) and (b) present, respectively, the normlized UV–visible spectra of pristine DBC molecules and N-doped derivatives as computed at the CAM-B3LYP/6–31++G(d,p) level of theory. As seen, each spectrum exhibits only one absorption band, mainly centered at 364, 293, and 290 nm for
DBC a
,
DBC b
, and
DBC c
, respectively, and at 511, 506, and 616 nm for
DBC a
-N,
DBC b
-N, and
DBC c
-N. Compared to pristine DBC molecules, the maximum absorption is red-shifted with the N-doping by 147, 213, and 326 nm for
DBC a
,
DBC b
, and
DBC c
, respectively.

As mentioned in Section 3.4 (see Table 2), the N-doping of the pristine DBC molecules reduces the HOMO-LUMO gap and increases the wavelength of absorption, which is consistent with the trend obtained in Fig. 7. The shape closely resembles that obtained experimentally by Kumar et al. [10] in dichloromethane. Furthermore, the maximum absorption wavelength of 379 nm is shifted by 15 nm compared to the calculations performed here in the gas phase using the CAM-B3LYP/6–31++G(d,p) level of theory. Previous studies have demonstrated that solvents have a negligible impact on absorption wavelengths [85,86]. Therefore, our predicted absorption wavelengths appear to be reliable based on these previous arguments.

As can be seen, the insertion of nitrogen induces a pronounced bathochromic shift, which is essential for the absorption of visible light. This leads to N-doped molecules that can be useful as optical transmission materials.

Optoelectronic properties like polarization density P, electrical susceptibility
χ e
, the relative dielectric permittivity
ε r
, the dielectric constant ε, the refractive index n, the volume V of the molecular structure, the radius of the molecule R, the phase velocity
V
p h
, the amplitude of the electric field E and magnetic B, the electric displacement vector
D →
are listed in Table 4 .

Comparing the computed values of E, ε, n, and D to those found in the literature [87]. Based on these findings, we deduced that doping these materials with nitrogen enhances the dynamics and charge distribution inside the molecules. Because of this, they are more likely to find use in optoelectronics, especially in the generation of organic light-emitting diodes, photonic devices, and photovoltaic cells using renewable energy sources. The electrical susceptibilities
χ e
for each molecule's corresponding DBCs-N 3.86, 4.02, and 4.63 more than 2 show a suitably quick response time and provide diversity in molecular design or technology. The doped molecular structures' refractive index values were 2.20 (
DBC a
-N), 2.24 (
DBC b
-N), and 2.37 (
DBC c
-N). These figures demonstrate a certain decrease in transparency when compared to glass (n = 1.5) [88], which serves as a reference material. By adding extra electrons or doping with a nitrogen atom, the doped systems developed here show increased refractive indices, confirming the loss of transparency. Thus, in such conditions, an incident electromagnetic wave propagates rapidly. Hence, these large refractive index and polarization values of molecule structures make them suitable for use in linear optical and photonic devices [88].

The energy derivation approach is utilized to calculate tensors, polarizabilities, and static hyperpolarizabilities from the optimized geometries, taking into account the cartesian coordinates of the electric field [89]. Table 5 contains the values of the tensors and means.

The presence of the nitrogen atom increases the value of the polarization parameter, which describes the linearity of a molecule's structure. Equations (1)–(4) were used as mathematical formulas to get these parameters. It is observed that for the nitrogen derivatives of the molecules under study, the dipole moment values are around 3 Debye. Therefore, it can be said that, in accordance with reports in the literature, these materials are polar [90,91]. Moreover, only the components xx and xxx have larger values when one looks at the six components of the tensor of asymmetric polarizability and the ten components of the tensor of hyperpolarizability of the first order. This leads one to believe that these parameters determine the linear and nonlinear characteristics of these molecules, respectively. But the DBCs-N had static polarizabilities α of 442.960, 443.523, and 455.657 au, and first order hyperpolarizabilities β of 2689.838, 5572.137, and 12717.472 au, respectively. Using para-nitroaniline (pNA α = 114.7 and β = 1072.44 au) as a reference organic molecule for nonlinear optical characteristics [92,93]. Thus, we find that both the first static hyperpolarizability β and the polarizability α are significantly increased by nitrogen doping. When an electron-donating group and an electron-withdrawing group interact via a system of conjugated double bonds, as in our systems under study, organic compounds with high hyperpolarizability are found. Our findings demonstrate the superior material properties of these doped molecules (
DBC a
-N,
DBC b
-N and
DBC c
-N) for nonlinear optic applications, including frequency shifting, optical modulation, optical communication, optical logic, and optical memories for emerging technologies in data storage, computing devices, signal processing, dynamic imaging, and optical connectors [94,95].

Table 6 lists the following: electron affinity (EA), ionization potential (IP), chemical potential (
μ c
), electronegativity (EN), chemical hardness (η), chemical softness (s), electrophilicity index (ω), maximum charge transferred (ΔN), and electron and hole reorganization energies (
λ e
and
λ h
).

Reorganization energy quantifies the transport properties of charge carriers [96]. For all the molecular structures studied, the reorganization energies of holes
λ h
are slightly higher than those of electrons
λ e
. These materials therefore have very good hole transfer capability that can be used in electroluminescent devices, as demonstrated in the work of Bo Chao Lin et al. [97]. In short, the structures we propose in this study are materials with interesting hole and electron reorganization energies. The electron affinity values of the neutral molecules are 0.93, 0.94, and 0.81
eV
, respectively, and those of the nitrogen-doped compounds are 1.72, 1.12, and 0.95
eV
, respectively, all of which are higher than those of the pristine molecules. The ionization potentials of the pristine systems are also 6.57, 6.56, and 6.70
eV
, and those of the nitrogen derivatives are 4.27, 4.78, and 4.84
eV
, respectively. All these values are lower than those of neutral molecules. We therefore observe an increase in electron affinity EA and a decrease in ionization potential IP in doped DBC-N systems compared with undoped DBCs. These values of electron affinity and ionization potential obtained are in good agreement with those of studies carried out by Robert G. Parr et al. [98] on certain reference molecules, such as perylene, with applications in organic electronics. Additionally, the ability of a chemical system to receive electrons is measured by the electrophilicity index (ω). A high electrophilicity index, therefore, indicates that a molecule is a good electrophile. A higher overall electrophilicity value indicates greater reactivity [40,98]. The
DBC a
-N compound is more reactive than the others due to its higher electrophilicity index value. Consequently, the electrophilicity index of
DBC a
-N was significantly impacted by N-doping, in contrast to
DBC b
-N and
DBC c
-N. This difference may be due to shape, edge, or steric hindrance effects, which are more pronounced in the
DBC b
-N and
DBC c
-N molecules than in the
DBC a
-N molecule. Thus, low chemical potential (
μ c
) and/or low chemical hardness (η) are indicative of a competent electrophile.

The most crucial factors that enable us to comprehend molecule interactions and provide an explanation for molecular structure and reactivity are the highest occupied HOMO and lowest vacant LUMO molecular orbitals [99]. Molecules that are nucleophilic, or electron donors, are represented by the symbol HOMO, whereas those that are electrophilic, or electron acceptors, are represented by the symbol LUMO. The HOMO and LUMO energy distributions and the three-dimensional active sites are shown in Fig. 8 . Surface areas that are negatively charged are represented by red lobes, and those that are positively charged by green lobes. Interestingly, nitrogen has a positive role in charge transfer to increase the electrical dynamism of these generated compounds. Thus, Fig. 8 shows that nitrogen is highly active in all HOMO and LUMO orbital diagrams of the N-doped structures, especially for the
DBC c
-N molecule, which supports its 1.65 eV low energy gap.

The molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) reactive sites of DBCs and their nitrogen derivatives are depicted in Fig. 9 with different surface colors: green denotes zero potential, blue indicates more positive potential, and red indicates more electronegative potential. The red and yellow patches, which surround the C–C and N–C bonds in the DBCs and DBCs-N structures, are regions of high electron density linked to electrophilic reactivity. Localized around C–H and N–H bonds in molecular structures, the blue areas signify low electron density and lean towards nucleophilic reactivity [100,101]. A propensity for electrophilic reactivity has been observed around the N atom. This demonstrates once more how the nitrogen atom affects the electrical potential. It is noteworthy that nearly every structure analyzed for DBCs has electrophilic sites at its core.

The excited state calculations allowed the density curves of the states to be drawn, which indicate how many states are available and likely to be occupied by the system at each energy level. Fig. 10 displays the spectra of the densities and gap energies of the molecules under study through the HOMO and LUMO energies of the corresponding molecular structures. The relationship between the gap energy and the system's electrical conductivity is σ ∝ exp(
−
E
g a p
2
k B
T
), where
k B
is the Boltzmann constant [102].

The band gap, which is represented by the gap energy, precisely corresponds to the zone situated between the occupied region (green region) and the virtual region (purple region). The number of electronic states N(E) with a certain energy E in the material under consideration is represented by the blue or red curve, known as the DOS (Density of States). These figures show a significant decrease in the prohibited band associated with the nitrogen atom present in the DBCs-N molecular configurations. The densities of states of our compounds correlate with energy levels such as HOMO and LUMO. This finding further demonstrates the significant effects of the N atom on tuning the electronic and structural properties of DBCs. It is important to note that the electrical conductivity of DBC-N materials is promoted by N doping. However, no further studies on the densities of states in these DBC models have been conducted to date.

Fig. 11 depicts the fluctuations in the examined molecules' standard thermodynamic parameters, such as entropy (S) and Gibbs free energy (G), throughout the temperature range of 298–800 K with a step of 100 K. It is evident that in the gas phase, entropy grows linearly with temperature. This outcome is consistent with other studies that have been published in the literature [103,104]. Additionally, we observe that the nitrogen-doped molecules have slightly more entropy than the undoped molecules. For all of our compounds, the Gibbs free energies are nearly constant between 298 and 800 K and decrease with doping with the nitrogen atom.

Using a combination of fundamental principles of density functional theory (DFT), this work provides a comprehensive theoretical investigation of the optoelectronic, nonlinear optical, and thermodynamic features of the examined systems. The study examined the effects of nitrogen dopant on several DBC structures, including gap energies, HOMO-LUMO molecular orbitals, IR and UV spectra, densities of states, and thermodynamic parameters. The observed results can offer useful direction for the synthesis and design of NLO materials based on GQDs produced from dibenzocoronene. These studies showed that the bandgap width of N-doped systems is greatly reduced by the nitrogen atom. The reason for this is that nitrogen has a higher electronegative potential than carbon. Moreover, the gap energy decreases while first-order polarizability and hyperpolarizability values rise with nitrogen doping. These compounds may be created according to an analysis of the thermodynamic properties of the doped molecules. The various characteristics that have been noted lead us to the conclusion that these nitrogen-doped molecules present a viable way to rationally build novel nonlinear optical materials.
